content.text: This site highlights a few of my past and ongoing computer science projects. For more information about myself or the code please visit my LinkedIn and GitHub.

This page highlights two computer graphics projects I worked on in college.
Both are created with C's OpenGL library. Project Aang focuses on creating 3D structures, while the second one focuses more on shaders.

Terrain was used as cliff walls, mountain peaks, and rock fields. It was generated using "random" height values for a 2D grid of points. Triangles filled in the grid. For the cliffs, multiple grids of terrian were generated and connected. The cliffs could have a dynamic number of sides. There should be 4, 5, and 6 sided cliffs in the screen shot. For the peaks of the mountains, one grid of terrain was used, but the random height function forced points closer to the center of the grid to have larger values

Bridges on the Eastern Air Temple had thier shape gernerated from two ellipses. A outer ellipse for the top and a inner ellipse for the bottom. Normals for the ellipse points were calculated using the line perpendicular to the tangent at that point. The tangent's slope comes from the derivative of the equation below.
A simple bar and campground were created using vert, geometry, and frag shaders.
A highlight in the bar is the stone bricks. A normal map was created to match the brick texture. Vert and Frag shaders then calculate lighting per pixel using the normal map.

Another highlight for this project was the fireflies. A geometry shader was used to convert a point into a square with a firefly texture texture on it. These squares would always face the scene's camera. The firefly would blend with the background to make it look translucent. For the bug's movement, a invisible box was created for them to bounce around in. Some randomization was added to their movement to make it floaty and life like. Logic was also added to have the bugs transition from lit to non-lit.

Another hightlight for this project was the lake's reflection. This was done by drawing the scene multiple times. The frames were then combined into a final frame the user sees. The first draw is from the scene's camera, where the surface of the lake is a green screen. The second draw is from the "virtual" camera location if we treat the lake as a flat mirror. For this draw, the lake has no surface so the camera can see through it. The green screen from frame one is then replaced by the its contents in frame two.

Using angular I built a MP3 Player application. The focus of this project was to learn Angular and practice building a complex app with it. I also practiced my UX skills by copying UX I liked in existing enterprise apps.
The Library, each Playlist, and an Album's song list are all the same angular component.
I created a player service which is a singleton that represents the current playing song, the song queue, and the controlls for each. Compontents accorss the app share this service.

Computers solve the intergral of a function in the same way to what you may have learned first in school. Since the intergral of a function is the area under the curve, the computer will approximate the area by covering it with a number of rectangles and calculating their areas instead.

I wrote the logic for determining what hand a user has. ...
This logic is used to help the user know their hand as they play it and to determine a winner by compairing hands against one another.
I wrote animation logic with in the WebGL logic to produce the deal, card flip, and peek animations.
